steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes a chain: SAGE uses longitudinal methods, which require extensive financial support. When causation is irrelevant, a study does not need longitudinal methods. The blank must complete the logical consequence of not needing those methods — specifically, what that means for cost.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the expense of SAGE is likely greater than the cost of longitudinal studies of sociology.': This compares SAGE to "longitudinal studies of sociology" — but the sociology study in the passage is explicitly not longitudinal. The comparison is to the wrong thing. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the success of the sociology study likely requires significantly less financial outlay than that needed for SAGE.': SAGE needs extensive money because it needs longitudinal methods. The sociology study doesn\'t need longitudinal methods, so it follows directly that it needs far less money. This is the correct consequence. ✓
  • Choice C — 'conclusions drawn from SAGE are likely to be more authoritative than those from the sociology study.': The passage is about cost and method necessity, not about which study produces more authoritative conclusions. This shifts to a different criterion not supported by the text. ✗
  • Choice D — 'longitudinal methods are suitable for studies of aging but ought to be avoided for those of sociology.': The passage says longitudinal methods are unnecessary when causation is irrelevant — not that they should be avoided in sociology generally. This overstates the claim. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
